title: Cantucci
subtitle: Tuscan twice-baked almond biscotti
excerpt: The dry, crunchy Tuscan biscuit baked twice to remove all moisture. Almonds throughout, scented with orange zest. Made for dipping in Vin Santo or strong coffee.
sourceNotes: Cantucci (also called biscotti di Prato) originate in the Tuscan city of Prato, where they have been produced continuously since at least the fourteenth century and are documented in the archives of the Arte dei Fornai (the bakers' guild). The twice-baked method (biscotto in Italian means 'twice cooked') removes all moisture, giving an extraordinary shelf life. Carol Field's The Italian Baker (1985) and Marcella Hazan's Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking (1992) both document the canonical recipe.

recipe.batchNotes: Scale freely. Cantucci keep for up to 3 weeks in a tin; a double batch is worthwhile.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cantucci improve over several days as they dry further. Store in an airtight tin and they will keep for 3 weeks.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; first bake 170°C, second bake 150°C

glossaryTerms.definition: A biscuit or bread baked first as a log or whole shape, then sliced and returned to the oven to dry out completely. The second bake removes residual moisture, producing a very crisp, long-keeping biscuit that softens only when dipped in liquid.

content.text: Cantucci are intentionally hard. They are not meant to be eaten dry in the hand. The tradition is to dip them in Vin Santo, the sweet Tuscan dessert wine, or in strong espresso, and eat them while they are briefly softened. Attempting to bite an unsoaked cantuccio risks teeth.

Leave the almonds whole. Chopped almonds give a uniform texture; whole almonds give the characteristic chunky cross-section where each slice reveals a different configuration of nuts. Toast them first to bring out their flavour.

Heat the oven to 170°C fan. Line a baking tray with baking paper. Whisk the {{eggs}}, {{caster-sugar}}, {{vanilla-extract}}, and {{orange}} zest together until pale and slightly thickened, about 2 minutes. Fold in the {{plain-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, and {{fine-sea-salt}}, then the toasted {{almonds}}. The dough will be sticky. With floured hands, shape into two logs about 25 cm long and 6 cm wide on the lined tray. Bake for 25 to 28 minutes until pale golden and set. Remove from the oven and leave to cool for 15 minutes.

Reduce the oven to 150°C fan. Using a sharp serrated knife, cut each log diagonally into slices about 1 cm thick. Lay the slices cut-side down on the baking tray. Return to the oven for 20 to 25 minutes, turning once halfway, until the
biscotti are completely dry and pale golden on both sides. Cool on a rack. They will harden completely as they cool.
